task.title: Phase 2 Exercise: Writing Your First DITA Topics
task.shortdesc: Practice DITA information typing by creating concept, task, and reference topics from scratch for a fictional product. Required for both technical writers and software engineers.

ul.li: Completed Phase 1 exercise (DITA-OT installed and working)
Read Part 3 topics: DITA Information Typing and DITA Authoring

context.p: You will create documentation for a fictional tool called TaskFlow — a command-line task manager. This gives you practice with all three core topic types.
context.note: About fictional products: The learner exercises use "TaskFlow" throughout Phases 1–5. The Part 3 reading uses "WidgetPro" in its inline tutorial. The XSLT labs in exercises/ use "AcmeDoc" as lab content. Each product gives you a fresh practice context for that segment of learning.

ul.li: Root element: <concept>
Include a <shortdesc> (1-2 sentences)
Include at least two <section> elements with titles
Use a <ul> (unordered list) for features
Include one <note type="tip">

info.p: Topic content idea: Explain what TaskFlow is, list 4-5 key features, and describe who should use it.

stepresult.p: Your file should validate as well-formed XML. Test by building:

ul.li: Root element: <task>
Include <prereq> with system requirements
Include <context> explaining what the user will accomplish
Write 4-6 <step> elements inside <steps>
At least one step must have <substeps>
At least two steps must have <stepresult>
Include a <result> section at the end

info.p: Topic content idea: Walk through downloading, extracting, configuring, and verifying TaskFlow installation.

stepresult.p: Build and verify the steps render as a numbered procedure in HTML5.

ul.li: Root element: <reference>
Include a <refbody>
Create a <table> with at least 3 columns (Command, Arguments, Description)
Include at least 5 rows of CLI commands
Add a <section> with <codeblock> showing usage examples

info.p: Topic content idea: Document TaskFlow commands like add, list, done, delete, export.

step.cmd: Build all three topics together to verify they are well-formed.

info.codeblock: # Build each individually to catch errors dita -i taskflow-docs/what-is-taskflow.dita -f html5 -o out/taskflow dita -i taskflow-docs/installing-taskflow.dita -f html5 -o out/taskflow dita -i taskflow-docs/cli-commands.dita -f html5 -o out/taskflow

stepresult.p: All three files should build without errors. Open each HTML file and verify:

ul.li: Concept: sections, lists, and note render correctly
Task: steps appear as numbered list with substeps indented
Reference: table renders with proper columns and rows

step.cmd: Reflect on your topic type choices.

info.p: Answer these questions in a comment at the top of each file:

ul.li: Why is "What is TaskFlow?" a concept and not a reference?
Could the CLI commands topic be a task instead? Why or why not?
What would you add to the installation task if it had to support 3 operating systems?

info.note: Writer focus: Pay attention to how each topic type constrains your writing — this structure is what makes DITA content reusable. Engineer focus: Notice how the DTD enforces element order — this is the schema validation that enables automated processing.

p.b: Self-Check — you should now be able to:

ul.li: ✓ Create valid concept, task, and reference topics from scratch
✓ Use semantic elements: section, steps, substeps, table, codeblock, note
✓ Choose the right topic type for different content purposes
✓ Build and view individual DITA topics as HTML5
